item.content:encoded: Nice! I'm very fond of Are We Cool Yet? as a GOI and have several plans for San Francisco Jones as a character in future tales.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know how I feel about AWCY being responsible for an attempt at removing skill or talent from art. If anything it should lead to an anartist civil war between AWCY and the people who don't have to ask.

Right from around their start I pretty much stood on the position that AWCY? did feature a strong actual political/activist current at least on its outskirts - an argument about this basically led to me writing the anomalous transmuting urinal.

This is another step in that direction and I totally approve.

item.title: Re: Of the people, by the people, for the people
item.content:encoded: Check out this fantastic Inktober sketch of 3360 created by the wonderful Zhange!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I definitely expected some level of characterization to Prole itself at first, but the ReadMe did well to establish why that wouldn't be happening.

Also, "you already are" is probably my favorite AWCY?-related line so far.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the ultimate studio assistant

give it to me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "ANTISTALLMAN" is a bit on-the-nose, and nothing in the testing log is particularly memorable (unfortunate given its length) but the core anomaly is fun to think about. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Liked it all the way up to the last few of lines of the note. The "are we cool yet" nod and wink felt perfunctory. If you took out everything between "art is cool" and "you already are" and ended it on "Stay Cool, San Francisco Jones." you could accomplish the same wink and nod without beating the reader over the head with it.

Then we got to the test logs, which felt tacked on. They have no real impact on the overall narrative of the piece and, ultimately, I think they distract from what the article is attempting to communicate to the reader. It doesn't help that they're not particularly outstanding tests; as nothing in them made me stand up and take notice (well, except maybe the Spore reference but that's just cause I still play that game sometimes).

Ultimately, you could end the whole piece on "stay cool" (while taking out the lines I suggested earlier) and end up with a much superior piece.

Not sure yet how my vote's gonna land, which probably means a no-vote. I liked it a lot up until how you ended it, which will likely prevent my downvote, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A noble AWCY object! What a surprise, and a good one too

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great. I now have an image of Pesterbot the Destroyer as a graffiti artist. Thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Art is cool. Prole is cool. And so is this SCP.

+1 ARD.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The closest an article has come to true literary greatness. A pitch perfect blend of social commentary and character, optimism and cynicism, gentle humour and depth, all without breaking tone or doing any fancy literary tricks.

+1 with extreme enthusiasm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really feel there was a missed opportunity here to give good characterization to the entity which has a decentralized identity, never being able to create its own art while always being filled with the ideas of other people. While I did enjoy the visceral and well-described imagery of the test logs it ultimately didn't do enough for me. I also like the note from San Francisco Jones but I think the content is less interesting than if you had put the effort into writing from the perspective of the SCP. It's well put together and enjoyable, but I still find it lacking. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This felt to me like another thing that makes other weird things. The extra bits of story were okay, but not enough to keep my interest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You had me at 'proletariart'. The rest was delicious lagniappe.
Wanting to make art, knowing how to make art, understanding what art even is - these are all issues of significance to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well that explains the questions in chat yesterday. Nice work man, really cool to see an accessibility focused anartist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is cool, and I appreciate the trend of not just having AWCY? be pretentious hacks because lol reasons. The very end of the note felt a bit repetitive, tho. Still, quite enjoyable. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really good! The idea of art being made by thought is an incredible idea, and you executed it very well. Especially with the idea of "Artists might be in everyone, but they just weren't able to become artists", that really draws sympathy to the SCP and its creator for simply wanting to do good.

Not to mention the surrealness of the blind D-Class's art piece, it feels very appropriate that a blind person would think in mostly blacks, but also that the piece itself has to do with running into things and shattering, almost as if it's a literal interpretation of how the blind person thinks of his blindness (A curse that causes me to run into things, and each time it feels worse and worse and I break more and more). Very well done! +1

item.title: Of the people, by the people, for the people
item.content:encoded:
I always wanted to make art. This is the closest I can get at this stage in my life.

Thanks to minmin, Roget, and ChaoSera for critique.

Thanks to trennerdios, ghostchibi, Gaffney, Modern_Erasmus, Perelka_L, and EldritchCadence for their test log suggestions.

See more of my work on my author page!
